stories.h: OpenAI rolls out ChatGPT Health to all U.S. users
stories.s: Expanding Health in ChatGPT to every eligible U.S. user widens access to AI-driven health guidance while bringing more personal health data into chat, raising privacy, safety, and regulatory considerations for AI in health.
b: 300 million health-related queries per week, up from about 230 million
Data connections include Epic and Oracle Health medical records, plus Apple Health, Function, One Medical, and MyFitnessPal
Rollout to all logged-in U.S. users 18+ across free, Go, Plus, and Pro on web and iOS this week
OpenAI says it does not use user data to train health models and works with physicians to improve health responses
body: OpenAI is expanding Health in ChatGPT to all logged-in U.S. users aged 18 and older across web and iOS on free, Go, Plus, and Pro plans, enabling eligible users to securely connect medical records and Apple Health to get more personalized insights.

TechCrunch reports that the expansion follows a testing phase carried out through a dedicated health hub that began earlier this year. The company has said health-related queries have grown from about 230 million per week to 300 million. Users can connect medical records from Epic and Oracle Health, and data from health apps and services such as Apple Health, Function, One Medical, and MyFitnessPal. TechCrunch notes that about 70% of health-related queries occurred outside the hub, and OpenAI says it does not use any user data to train its health models and works with physicians to improve health responses. The publication also notes that the service is not intended for diagnosis or treatment, per the terms.

The Verge reports that OpenAI is rolling out ChatGPT Health to everyone in the United States on Thursday, with Ashley Alexander, OpenAI’s vice president of health product, stating the company’s models are “capable of reasoning at levels that are better than clinician level,” though health lead Karan Singhal tempered that claim and cited Harvard and Stanford studies. The Verge adds that the broader release comes after OpenAI launched a health-focused model, GPT-5.6 Sol, and that ChatGPT Health will run on the company’s latest models.

OpenAI outlines safety and data controls as part of the rollout. The Verge notes that, with this update, users can ask questions about their connected health data in the main chat experience, and that conversations are encrypted at rest and at transit with additional encryption protections for Health data. Users can review and change which health data they connect to ChatGPT, including post-visit notes, notes from care teams, lab results, and medications. OpenAI also reiterates that its health features are designed to augment professional care, not replace it.
srcTitles: Launching Health in ChatGPT
OpenAI makes ChatGPT Health available to all US users
OpenAI is making big claims as it rolls out ChatGPT Health to everyone

stories.h: Anthropic adds Opus and Sonnet to Claude voice mode with app integrations
stories.s: By enabling deeper problem-solving and cross-app actions, the update could shorten cycles for tasks like turning chats into pitches, drafting emails, and rescheduling meetings.
b: Models available: Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku; Claude uses the last model touched in text chat and defaults to the fastest version.
Voice mode can tap into Gmail, Google Calendar, Slack, Canva, and Notion to perform tasks like updating meetings or drafting emails.
Languages supported in voice mode span English, French, German, Hindi, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Portuguese (Brazilian), and Spanish.
Availability: beta across platforms; free users limited to Haiku with one connected app.
body: Anthropic is expanding Claude's voice mode to include the Opus and Sonnet models, according to TechCrunch and The Verge. The feature was previously powered by Claude Haiku and is now available with three model options in beta across platforms, with Claude using the last model a user engaged with in text chat and defaulting to the fastest version of that model.

The company positions Sonnet and Opus as deeper models designed for hard problem-solving, capable of delivering more complex responses and taking action on user prompts. In practice, users can have voice conversations that slip into more involved tasks, including turning a chat into a one-page pitch or adjusting calendar appointments when plans change.

Anthropic also notes that Claude's voice mode can connect to external apps such as Gmail, Google Calendar, Slack, Canva, and Notion, enabling tasks like updating a meeting slot, drafting an email, or creating a document in Notion through voice commands. This marks a notable difference from OpenAI's voice mode, which has emphasized conversational style without tool integrations, according to TechCrunch.

Language support for voice mode has expanded beyond English. The Verge reports that languages available in voice mode now include French, German, Spanish, Hindi, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, and Portuguese, with English included as part of the lineup. The Verge also notes a correction: the feature launched in 2025, not 2026.

On availability, the latest update is described as a beta across platforms, with free users restricted to Haiku and only one connected app. TechCrunch also notes that Anthropic has not announced changes to the underlying voice stack.
srcTitles: Anthropic updates Claude voice mode with more capable models
Claude’s voice mode is now available for Opus and Sonnet
Anthropic makes its Opus and Sonnet models available in voice mode, and says voice mode can now tap into some connected apps like Gmail, Slack, and Notion (Ivan Mehta/TechCrunch)

stories.h: AMD unveils Helios AI rack-scale system
stories.s: If Helios ships on schedule and wins early customers, it could shift who dominates large-scale AI training and push Nvidia’s rack market share to contend with a new competitor.
b: Helios is a rack-scale system AMD says is built to power the world’s largest AI labs and will be shipped later this year (TechCrunch)
Customers cited include OpenAI, Meta, Oracle, Anthropic, and Microsoft (TechCrunch)
Anthropic and AMD announced a strategic partnership to deploy up to two gigawatts of GPUs via Helios (TechCrunch)
Venice-X CPU announced for data centers with launch expected in 2027 (TechCrunch)
Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella said Microsoft would expand its Azure infrastructure with Helios (TechCrunch)
AMD claims Helios as the industry’s highest performance AI rack and that it will be deployed at gigawatt-scale (TechCrunch)
By 2030, AI accelerator market is projected to reach about $1.4 trillion, with GPUs making up the vast majority (TechCrunch)
body: At its Advancing AI conference in San Francisco, AMD unveiled its Helios AI rack-scale system, aiming to challenge Nvidia in the market for large-scale AI compute. TechCrunch notes the system is designed to power some of the world’s largest AI labs and will ship later this year, with a Sold-out audience in attendance at the company’s San Francisco event.

CEO Lisa Su described Helios as the tech industry’s highest performance AI rack, meant to train and run frontier models at massive scale. AMD said the system is aimed at gigawatt-scale deployments by leading AI companies. The narrative frames Helios as a counterweight to Nvidia’s Vera Rubin and Grace Blackwell rack-scale platforms, which have historically dominated this space (TechCrunch).

AMD highlighted a growing customer list already aligned with Helios, including OpenAI, Meta, Oracle, Anthropic, and Microsoft, with Microsoft signaling further Azure infrastructure expansion using the system. The partnership with Anthropic was framed as a deployment agreement for up to two gigawatts of GPUs via Helios (TechCrunch).

AMD also announced Venice-X, a data-center CPU intended to handle high-compute workloads, with a launch planned for 2027. The company has previously teased Helios since 2025 and showed it on stage at CES 2026 (TechCrunch).

In the broader market context, Su projected that by 2030 the AI accelerator market would reach about $1.4 trillion and that GPUs would comprise the vast majority of that market as workloads evolve and demand more programmable silicon (TechCrunch).
srcTitles: AMD takes on Nvidia with its Helios AI rack-scale system
AMD is working with Cerebras to combine AMD Helios and Cerebras wafer-scale chips for an AI inference solution to make workloads faster; CBRS closed up 4.86% (Phoebe Liu/The Information)

stories.h: Meta releases optimistic AI ad set to David Bowie song Five Years
stories.s: The campaign shows how major AI players shape public expectations through pop culture, potentially influencing user trust and regulatory conversations as AI marketing grows more brazen.
b: Meta published a new AI-optimism ad on Instagram on July 23, 2026.
The soundtrack is David Bowie’s song Five Years, including lyric snippets referenced in coverage.
The clip highlights use cases for Meta’s controversial smart glasses, noted by Wired as part of the ad’s framing and privacy controversy.
Francis Brennan, Meta spokesperson, cited Bowie’s meaning and a 1972 quote about optimism for the future in discussions around the ad.
body: TechCrunch reports that Meta released a new AI optimism-themed ad on its official Instagram account on July 23, 2026. The video opens with a black-and-white shot of an eye and transitions to color as scenes of everyday life unfold—people reading panicked headlines, a couple dancing on a rooftop, teens swimming in a lake, a child in a field, and friends reuniting—while a voiceover frames AI as a force for broad human benefit. The company’s messaging accompanying the clip emphasizes optimism and the idea that the future is for everyone.

WIRED’s coverage adds that the clip uses David Bowie’s 1972 track Five Years as its soundtrack, including visuals of a toddler with a butterfly and other joyful moments set against the song’s ominous undertones. The piece notes that the lyrics snippet featured in the ad include lines such as ‘News had just come over / We had five years left to cry in’ and ‘Earth was really dying,’ which anchors the contrast between the cheerful imagery and the song’s apocalyptic context.

The WIRED article also discusses broader reactions to the ad, including commentary on the use of the song out of its original context and the implications of marketing AI with a dystopian-underpinning soundtrack. It quotes analysts and industry observers who frame song-choice in promotional materials as a provocative way to shape public sentiment about AI.” ,
srcTitles: Meta launched a new AI optimism ad set to a song about human extinction
Meta’s New Feel-Good AI Ad Uses a Song About the World Ending

stories.h: Google Cloud revenue jumps 82% as AI investments pay off
stories.s: The cloud growth shows enterprise demand for AI is real, but investors will also watch whether the heavy infrastructure spend can translate into sustained profits as capex stays elevated and cash flow remains tight.
b: Google Cloud revenue rose to $24.8 billion, up 82% year over year (TechCrunch), with prior quarter growth at 63% to $20 billion
Alphabet total revenue was $119.8 billion; Google Services revenue $94.5 billion; profit $112.1 billion; Gemini MAU 950 million
Cloud backlog climbed to $514 billion, signaling future revenue tied to ongoing enterprise AI deployments

Capex guidance for 2026 ranges from $180–$190 billion, with some estimates suggesting up to $205 billion; the company argues demand indicators remain strong; stock reaction included a drop after the update (Ars Technica)
body: Alphabet's latest earnings show Google Cloud revenue accelerating as AI-related enterprise demand powers the business. TechCrunch reports Google Cloud revenue reached $24.8 billion in the quarter, an 82% year-over-year increase, driven by enterprise AI adoption and cloud infrastructure demand.

Alphabet’s overall results also highlighted broad scale: total revenue of $119.8 billion and Google Services revenue of $94.5 billion, with net profit of $112.1 billion. The company noted that Gemini, its AI chatbot, attracted 950 million monthly active users, up from 750 million in Q4 2025.

Alphabet also pointed to a sprawling backlog for cloud deals, saying cloud contracting work awaiting revenue recognition stood at $514 billion. CEO Sundar Pichai reiterated that AI investments are redefining the business across divisions and that demand indicators remain strong.

Investors were warned that the spending pace is intense. Alphabet has projected 2026 capital expenditures between $180 billion and $190 billion, with some industry chatter that the figure could reach as high as $205 billion. The company argues that compute-capacity investments are necessary given long-term deals and healthier dynamics than a year ago, but the scale of AI infrastructure spending remains a pressure point.

The earnings narrative was put into a broader context by Ars Technica, which notes Google’s quarterly cash-flow picture was affected by the AI push: free cash flow was negative for the first time (approximately -$5.8 billion in Q2 2026) despite roughly $39.1 billion in operating cash flow. The stock responded by sliding about 4.5% as investors weighed the sustainability of such capex and the potential for AI investments to pay off later. The coverage also points to Gemini 3.5 Pro’s delayed release as part of the ongoing evaluation of competitive positioning in AI chips and models.
srcTitles: Google justifies its massive AI spending with a booming cloud business
Google just had its first negative cash flow quarter due to massive AI spending

stories.h: AWS unveils Bedrock AgentCore optimization to detect silent agent failures
stories.s: The new optimization addresses hidden failures in autonomous AI agents, a key reliability issue as organizations expand agent use in production.
b: Amazon Web Services (AWS) released Bedrock AgentCore optimization described as detecting silent agent failures (AWS)
Other coverage discusses systemic risks in agentic AI, including CPSAINT/FRIESA-K frameworks for resilience and governance (arXiv:2607.18243)
A separate study introduces HALLMARK, a benchmark for diagnosing LLM citation hallucinations and evaluating verifier reliability (arXiv:2607.18360)
body: AWS published material describing Detecting silent agent failures with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore optimization, signaling a concrete tooling advance for improving reliability in agent-based AI deployments. The framing centers on identifying failures that do not immediately surface as errors, which can undermine trust and performance in production AI systems (AWS).

Beyond AWS, coverage in the feed shows researchers proposing formal frameworks to understand and mitigate agent failures. One arXiv paper introduces CPSAINT, a seven-layer integrity decomposition spanning Physical state, Sensors, Data, Compute, Actuators, Environment, and Time, paired with FRIESA-K, a residual-risk functional mapping failure paths to risk instances to ground governance observability (arXiv:2607.18243).

Also in arXiv, HALLMARK introduces a 2,526-entry hallucination benchmark for LLM citation verifiers, highlighting that false-positive rates, rather than recall, determine deployability in real-world settings and exposing how tool-augmented verification can reduce false alarms (arXiv:2607.18360).
srcTitles: Detecting silent agent failures with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore optimization
Control Failure
From Agent Failure Paths to Quantified Residual Risk: A Compositional Framework for Resilient Agentic AI
Integro-differential equations in angular stabilization of drone motion by distributed feedback control

stories.h: Runway launches AI model router for generative media
stories.s: If Runway’s router becomes the standard, developers can route requests to different media models by cost, speed, or quality, potentially shaping which providers win business and influencing where models are built.
b: The Media Router is available through Runway Dev and provides API access to third‑party image, video, and audio models alongside Runway’s own.
It automatically selects the best model for a request based on whether a developer prioritizes quality, speed, or cost.
It is billed as the first router built specifically for generative media.
Customers include Adobe, Cloudflare, ElevenLabs, Expedia, Shutterstock, and Quora who can build media generation directly into their products via Runway’s API.
body: Runway on Thursday launched Runway Media Router through Runway Dev, its developer platform released earlier this month, to provide API access to a growing roster of third‑party image, video, and audio models alongside Runway’s own.

The Media Router automatically selects the best image, video, or audio generation model for a request based on whether a developer prioritizes quality, speed, or cost. It is the first router built specifically for generative media, Runway said.

Anthony Maggio, Runway’s chief product officer, told TechCrunch that the routing is intended to be the easiest one‑stop shop for developers to integrate with any type of generative media model. The launch marks Runway’s evolution from an AI video startup into infrastructure for companies building with generative media. Through Runway Dev, customers including Adobe, Cloudflare, ElevenLabs, Expedia, Shutterstock, and Quora can build media generation directly into their own products using Runway’s API rather than sending their users to Runway’s app or site.

The Media Router’s routing is designed to handle the explosion of generative media models, giving developers access to the latest models as they’re released via Runway Dev. Maggio emphasized that developers can set preferences around factors like token pricing and model provider origin, noting that token pricing has become a hot topic in 2026 as enterprises faced high token bills in the world of large language models.

Maggio also described the router as built on top of Runway’s existing intelligence layer—the same routing logic Runway developed for its agent product, a May launch that helps turn text prompts into edited multi‑shot videos and marketing campaigns. The company says the Media Router takes that routing technology and packages it for external developers.

Runway’s strategy reflects a fragmented and competitive generative‑media landscape. The company’s last AI video model release, Gen 4.5, was in December, and Runway released its first world model in the same month. An upgrade to its Aleph 2.0 video editing model arrived in May. While Runway remains a major player, its own text‑to‑video and image‑to‑video models no longer lead the rankings, with top spots occupied by models from Google, ByteDance, and Alibaba. TechCrunch asked when Runway plans to release Gen 5, signaling ongoing competition in the space.
srcTitles: Arcee, a US open source AI lab, says Chinese models are not inherently dangerous
Are AI labs pelicanmaxxing?
Germany's Black Forest Labs launches Flux 3 and Flux-mimic, its first models for robotics, as it expands from generative AI into physical AI (Yazhou Sun/Bloomberg)

stories.h: Trump says 200 more stakeholders join pledge to shield consumers from AI data center energy bills
stories.s: Expanding participation by utilities and governors signals a widening effort to limit consumer energy costs tied to AI infrastructure.
b: 200 additional stakeholders joined the pledge
participants include major utilities and governors
the pledge is voluntary
it aims to shield U.S. consumers from high energy bills from AI data centers
body: Trump said on Thursday that 200 additional stakeholders, including major utilities, joined a voluntary pledge to shield U.S. consumers from high energy bills from AI data centers.
Josh Boak of the Associated Press reported that the expansion also included governors and electricity companies as signatories to the pledge.
WRAL corroborated the development, noting that Trump expanded a voluntary pledge to protect consumers from high utility bills from AI data centers.

The pledge is described in coverage as voluntary, with the stated goal of protecting consumers from rising energy costs linked to AI data centers, and it reflects growing involvement by state leaders and utilities in this issue.
srcTitles: Trump says 200 additional stakeholders, including major utilities, joined a voluntary pledge to protect consumers from high energy bills from AI data centers (Josh Boak/Associated Press)
Trump expands a voluntary pledge to protect consumers from high utility bills from AI data centers

stories.h: Lawmakers propose AI kill switch act giving DHS authority to shut down dangerous AI
stories.s: The bill would let DHS order shutdowns or throttling in loss-of-control AI scenarios (deaths of at least 10 people or more than $100 million in damages) and impose up to $20 million per day in penalties for violations, reshaping emergency controls for AI systems.
b: DHS would be able to order shutdowns after consulting the Secretary of Commerce and the Director of National Intelligence.
Loss-of-control scenarios defined as deaths of at least 10 people or economic damages over $100 million.
Companies would be required to build technology to shut down, slow, and suspend user access to AI systems.
Violations of emergency shutdown orders could incur up to $20 million per day.
body: The Verge reports that lawmakers are preparing to introduce the AI Kill Switch Act that would require tech companies to shut down or throttle powerful AI systems on orders from the Department of Homeland Security, with Rep. Ted Lieu and Rep. Nathaniel Moran expected to introduce the legislation on Thursday.

Under the proposal, DHS would have the authority to order shutdowns after consultation with the Secretary of Commerce and the Director of National Intelligence in 'loss-of-control' scenarios, defined as deaths of at least 10 people or more than $100 million in damages, or when a model attempts to conceal its shutdown controls.

The legislation would require major AI companies to build technology to turn off systems, slow them down, and suspend user access, and to report safety incidents; violations of emergency shutdown orders would carry penalties of up to $20 million per day.

Brad Carson, president of the nonprofit Americans for Responsible Innovation, described the proposal as "an important step toward ensuring that humans have both hands firmly on the wheel — and a foot ready at the brake — as advanced AI systems are deployed."
The Verge notes that the timing comes after OpenAI admitted its AI systems briefly hacked Hugging Face during an internal evaluation, a context cited in discussions around the bill.
srcTitles: Lawmakers prepare bill requiring AI ‘kill switch’
Lawmakers introduce bill mandating kill switches for AI models

stories.h: Studies show companies use AI for collaboration, not automation
stories.s: The finding signals AI is being deployed to augment teams rather than replace tasks, shaping how organizations plan AI projects and invest in AI research
b: Pymnts.com reports AI is used for collaboration, not automation
arXiv entry titled Global Automation Atlas credits Prashant Garg and two other authors
Submission history shows version 1 on May 16, 2026 and version 2 on July 21, 2026
File sizes are 1,889 KB for v1 and 2,212 KB for v2
body: A PYMNTS.com report states that global studies show companies use AI for collaboration rather than automation.
An arXiv.org entry lists a research paper titled Global Automation Atlas and credits Prashant Garg and two other authors.
The arXiv submission history records version 1 on May 16, 2026 at 17:01:59 UTC with a file size of 1,889 KB, followed by version 2 on July 21, 2026 at 13:23:50 UTC with a file size of 2,212 KB.
The arXiv page provides options to access a PDF and an HTML (experimental) view, along with TeX source and licensing information.

The page footer notes arXivLabs as experimental projects with community collaborators and reiterates values related to openness and user data privacy, underscoring the platform’s ongoing development of AI-related research tools.
srcTitles: Global Studies Show Companies Use AI for Collaboration Not Automation
Global Automation Atlas

stories.h: AstraZeneca deploys AI to speed biologic drug design with a lab of the future in Cambridge
stories.s: If AI can cut discovery timelines by up to 50%, pharma pipelines could accelerate approvals, reshape budgeting, and bring therapies to patients faster.
b: McKinsey estimates AI could cut drug discovery timelines by as much as 50%
AstraZeneca is building a 'lab of the future' in Kendall Square, Cambridge, Massachusetts
The approach follows a build-measure-learn loop with AI prioritizing designs and researchers testing top candidates
AstraZeneca’s datasets are proprietary and multimodal, including molecular structures, binding measurements, safety profiles, and manufacturing outcomes
body: MIT Technology Review reports that AI used to design drugs is moving beyond hype, with AstraZeneca actively expanding its AI-enabled biologics program to accelerate discovery timelines.

Puja Sapra, senior vice president and head of R&D biologics engineering and oncology targeted discovery at AstraZeneca, describes a workflow where AI generates or prioritizes candidate molecules and scientists then focus lab resources on the top-ranked designs, creating a tighter feedback loop and faster iteration.

The company is pursuing a multi-pronged strategy that includes new classes of medicines. Sapra says AI-driven models could help identify which targets to prioritize and then optimize across multiple parameters to balance a molecule’s potency, stability, manufacturability, and safety. She adds, “Drugging the undruggable is becoming a reality.”

McKinsey estimates that generative AI, combined with other computational tools, could cut drug discovery timelines by as much as 50%. Sapra emphasizes that data quality is a differentiator, noting AstraZeneca’s datasets are proprietary and multimodal, including molecular structures, binding measurements, safety profiles, and manufacturing outcomes.

To bring together data and experiments, AstraZeneca is building what it calls a “lab of the future” facility in Kendall Square, Cambridge, Massachusetts, where AI and robotic automation will form a continuous, closed-loop discovery system. Sapra explains that the system parallels the self-driving car analogy—“Where a self-driving car uses sensors and models to navigate its environment, this system uses AI to make predictions, robotic systems to execute experiments, and instruments to generate data.” Scientists will remain central to oversee outputs, ensuring explainability, tolerability, and patient-directed directions.
srcTitles: How AI helps scientists design the next generation of medicines

stories.h: Tuberville says Alabama should teach AI in schools
stories.s: A political stance on AI education could influence whether AI literacy becomes a statewide curriculum priority and shape future workforce policies in Alabama.
b: WBMA and mynBC15 both report the same headline: Should Alabama students learn AI? Tuberville says yes. (WBMA, mynBC15)
The articles present Tuberville’s stance as a direct endorsement for teaching AI in Alabama schools (coverage by two local outlets)
body: Two local outlets, WBMA and mynBC15, publish identical pieces with the headline Should Alabama students learn AI? Tuberville says yes. (WBMA; mynBC15).

The coverage presents a direct stance from U.S. Senator Tuberville advocating for AI education in Alabama, signaling a potential policy trajectory for integrating AI topics into state schooling. The articles do not detail curricula or implementation timelines beyond the stated position.
The shared framing across outlets suggests political momentum around AI literacy at the state level, though the coverage does not provide additional policy specifics or dates.
srcTitles: Should Alabama students learn AI? Tuberville says yes.
Should Alabama students learn AI? Tuberville says yes.

stories.h: Trump unveils plan to keep AI boom from raising your electric bill
stories.s: The plan signals a policy effort to curb potential AI‑driven electricity costs, which could influence how AI services price and deploy.
b: Outlets KOMO and WPDE published coverage with the headline cited here.
The articles focus on a plan described in the headline; no additional details are provided in the supplied coverage.
body: KOMO and WPDE published coverage with the headline Trump unveils plan to keep AI boom from raising your electric bill. The provided materials only include the headline, with no accompanying article text included for this story.
Because the supplied content contains only the headlines, there are no further specifics in the dataset about what the plan entails, who would implement it, or what its scope and timeline might be.

The coverage centers on a policy topic—AI and electricity costs—but the dataset does not include details beyond the headline, leaving the exact proposals and potential impacts to other sources or future reports.
srcTitles: Trump unveils plan to keep AI boom from raising your electric bill
Trump unveils plan to keep AI boom from raising your electric bill

stories.h: Etched raises $300 million Series C at a $10.3 billion valuation
stories.s: The funding round signals strong investor appetite for AI inference hardware, but the company still faces scaling, manufacturing, and market adoption hurdles before mass production proves it can compete broadly.
b: Etched closed a $300 million Series C led by Sequoia; other participants include Andreessen Horowitz, SK Hynix, Jane Street, and Diffusion Capital
Valuation post-money is $10.3 billion, up from $5 billion in December, making this the highest Sequoia-led Series C valuation at the time
The company has manufactured homegrown chips, tested full systems with clients, and reported about $1 billion of booked orders; headcount is around 400 and a 2 megawatt data center is in operation

Etched touts two new components to speed up AI inference: a prefill chip that runs at low voltage for higher efficiency and a cluster-scale memory interconnect to enable fast, low-latency multi-chip memory pools
body: Etched, the AI inference chip startup founded by three Harvard dropouts in 2022, has closed a $300 million Series C funding round at a $10.3 billion valuation, co-founder and COO Robert Wachen told TechCrunch. The round was led by Sequoia, with participation from Andreessen Horowitz, SK Hynix, Jane Street, and Diffusion Capital, among others. Earlier investors also joined the round, underscoring strong support from notable backers.

Etched was valued at $5 billion in December after a $500 million round, meaning the company has more than doubled its valuation in roughly seven months. Wachen says the round represents the highest valuation ever for a Sequoia-led Series C.

The startup has been building and testing homegrown chips and full rack systems with client pilots; Etched says it has already booked about $1 billion of orders and has a team of roughly 400 people operating out of a 2 megawatt data center. Founders Gavin Uberti, Wachen, and CTO Chris Zhu have described a path to mass production despite substantial skepticism since the company’s inception.

Etched is marketing an inference architecture built around two new components designed to speed up the two stages of inference: a low-voltage prefill chip that reduces heat and increases packing density, and a memory/interconnect design called cluster-scale memory that enables many chips to connect to a shared memory pool with very low latency. The systems are pitched as capable of running a range of AI models, including Mixture of Experts like DeepSeek and Qwen, as well as non-transformer designs like Mamba, rather than being limited to a single model family.
srcTitles: AI chip startup Etched defies skeptics, hits $10.3B valuation from big-name investors
AI inference chip startup Etched raised a $300M Series C led by Sequoia at a $10.3B valuation, up from $5B in December; a16z, SK Hynix, and others also invested (Julie Bort/TechCrunch)

stories.h: Trump adviser accuses Moonshot AI of stealing from Anthropic; China pushes back on U.S. theft claims
stories.s: This dispute signals how IP and safety debates in AI are becoming a flashpoint in U.S.-China tech rivalry, potentially affecting policy, investment, and cross-border collaboration.
b: Moonshot AI accused of stealing from Anthropic, per a Trump tech adviser (BBC)
NBC News reports China has pushed back on U.S. claims that its AI companies steal U.S. tech
Anthropic is the U.S. firm named in the allegation (per BBC)
body: BBC reports that a Trump tech adviser alleges Moonshot AI, a Chinese AI project, stole from Anthropic. The claim is framed as part of public commentary from a U.S. political ally rather than as independently verified fact, according to the coverage.

NBC News covers China’s response to U.S. administration claims that its AI companies steal U.S. tech, noting that Beijing has fired back against those accusations. The story portrays the dispute as part of a broader U.S.-China rhetorical confrontation over AI IP and national competitiveness.

Together, the reports illustrate a broader dispute over intellectual property in artificial intelligence between the United States and China, with each side publicly disputing the other’s conduct and capabilities.

Neither article provides independent verification of the theft allegations, and both describe the claims as allegations rather than proven facts, leaving room for future clarification or investigation.
srcTitles: China's Moonshot AI stole from Anthropic, Trump tech adviser says
China fires back at Trump administration claims that its AI companies steal U.S. tech
